coredata.dc:title: NH2-MIL-125(Ti) encapsulated with in situ-formed carbon nanodots with up-conversion effect for improving photocatalytic NO removal and H2 evolution
coredata.dc:creator: He, Youzhou
Luo, Shuang
Hu, Xueli
Cheng, Yaling
Huang, Yumin
Chen, Shengming
Fu, Min
Jia, Yiming
Liu, Xingyan
coredata.dc:description: The bifunctional photocatalyst CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti), carbon nanodots (CDs) encapsulated in NH2-MIL-125(Ti), was constructed in situ by a simple and convenient low-temperature calcination approach, which exhibited great capacity in NO removal (at air level) and H2 evolution simultaneously under visible-light irradiation. The NO removal rate of CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) (10 mM) reached 53%, which was 28.4% higher than pristine NH2-MIL-125(Ti). Moreover, the H2 production rate was 5820.95 μmol g−1, which was 5.34 times higher than that of original NH2-MIL-125(Ti). The corresponding optical and electrical performances of CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) characterized by UV–Vis, UCPL, ESR, and in situ DRIFTS, etc. indicated that the encapsulated CDs uniformly dispersed in the pores of NH2-MIL-125(Ti) could improve the visible light utilization via the up-conversion effect and photosensitization, and serve as an electron-receiver to significantly prolong the lifetime of the photogenerated charge carriers, thus enhancing the photocatalytic performance. The results revealed that fabricating CDs with metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) could be an available strategy for us to boost the photocatalytic NO elimination and H2 evolution activity simultaneously.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Bifunctional photocatalyst
Up-conversion effect
NO removal
Hydrogen evolution

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21502012 , 21805027 ), the Chongqing Science and Technology Commission ( cstc2018jcyjAX0531 , cstc2017jcyjAX0404 ), the Scientific and Technological Research Program of Chongqing Municipal Education Commission (KJQN201800836, KJQN201900838, KJ1500615, KJ1706174), the Funding Project for Scientific Research of Chongqing Technology and Business University (1752015, 1952004), the Innovation group of new technologies for industrial pollution control of Chongqing Education Commission (CXQT19023), the program of China Scholarships Council (201906075005), the Chongqing Key Laboratory of Catalysis and New Environmental Materials (KFJJ2019084), the Chongqing College Students’ Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program (S201911799027).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of NH2-MIL-125(Ti) and CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti); (b) FT-IR spectra of CDs, NH2-MIL-125(Ti) and CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti).

SEM of (a) CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) (10 mM); (b) TEM of CDs; (c) EDX mapping images of CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) (10 mM); EDX point scan of the surface of (d) NH2-MIL-125(Ti) and (e) CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) (10 mM).
XPS spectra of NH2-MIL-125(Ti) and CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) (10 mM), (a) C1s, (b) N1s.
(a) UV-Vis spectra of NH2-MIL-125(Ti) and CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti), (b) plot of the (αhv)2 versus photon energy (hv) for NH2-MIL-125(Ti) and CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti).

(a) PL spectra of NH2-MIL-125(Ti) and CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti); (b) EIS spectra of NH2-MIL-125(Ti) and CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti); (c) The transient photocurrents of NH2-MIL-125(Ti) and CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti); (d) LSV curves of NH2-MIL-125(Ti) and CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti).
UCPL spectra of the CDs under different excitation wavelengths.

(a) Photocatalytic activity of NO removal; (b) The cycling test of CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti); (c) Photocatalytic H2 generation rates for NH2-MIL-125(Ti) and CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) under visible light irradiatio; (d) Effects of different calcination temperatures of CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) (10 mM) on photocatalytic hydrogen production.
In situ DRIFTS spectra of photocatalytic NO oxidation on NH2-MIL-125(Ti) and CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) under visible light illumination and an oxygen atmospher.

DMPO spin-trapping ESR spectra of as-synthesized NH2-MIL-125(Ti) and CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) in the dark and under visible light irradiation for (a)
O2
− and (b)
OH.
Schematic illustration for the synthesis of CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti).
A possible photocatalytic mechanism for efficient hydrogen production and removal of NO of CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) under light irradiation.

head.ce:title: NH2-MIL-125(Ti) encapsulated with in situ-formed carbon nanodots with up-conversion effect for improving photocatalytic NO removal and H2 evolution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Catalysis and New Environmental Materials, College of Environment and Resources, Chongqing Technology and Business University, Chongqing 400067, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Catalysis and New Environmental Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials and Energy, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Chengdu 611731, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Electronic Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials and Energy, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Chengdu 611731, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry “G. Ciamician”, University of Bologna, Ravenna Campus, 48121 Ravenna, Italy
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry “G. Ciamician”, University of Bologna, Ravenna Campus, 48121 Ravenna, Italy

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Chongqing Technology and Business University, Xuefu Avenue, Nan'an District, Chongqing 400067, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The bifunctional photocatalyst CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti), carbon nanodots (CDs) encapsulated in NH2-MIL-125(Ti), was constructed in situ by a simple and convenient low-temperature calcination approach, which exhibited great capacity in NO removal (at air level) and H2 evolution simultaneously under visible-light irradiation. The NO removal rate of CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) (10 mM) reached 53%, which was 28.4% higher than pristine NH2-MIL-125(Ti). Moreover, the H2 production rate was 5820.95 μmol g−1, which was 5.34 times higher than that of original NH2-MIL-125(Ti). The corresponding optical and electrical performances of CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) characterized by UV–Vis, UCPL, ESR, and in situ DRIFTS, etc. indicated that the encapsulated CDs uniformly dispersed in the pores of NH2-MIL-125(Ti) could improve the visible light utilization via the up-conversion effect and photosensitization, and serve as an electron-receiver to significantly prolong the lifetime of the photogenerated charge carriers, thus enhancing the photocatalytic performance. The results revealed that fabricating CDs with metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) could be an available strategy for us to boost the photocatalytic NO elimination and H2 evolution activity simultaneously.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, theserious environmental pollution and increasing energy crisis have greatly promoted the development of photocatalytic technology, a clean and effective means to solve environmental and energy issues [1,2]. Among the various control methods for solving environmental pollution, the adoption of photocatalytic degradation of pollutants (especially nitrogen oxides, the main source of air pollution) provides a new avenue to solve environmental problems to the greatest extent [3], which is considered to be one of the ideal methods with inexhaustible and renewable solar energy. Relative to solar energy, hydrogen is another cleanand alternative energy source that has been highly expected to ease the energy crisis [4]. At present, there are several widely used approaches such as electrolysis and photolysis of water to obtain hydrogen source [5,6]. Among them, photolysis of water, a low-cost, pollution-free and sustainable approach, is considered to be an ideal hydrogen production method [7–9]. For both environmental restoration and energy production [10,11], the kind of photocatalysis material is the most important core-binding factor, which still has several defects such as poor light stability, weak light response ability and easy electron-hole recombination. Therefore, it is very necessary here to find an efficient photocatalyst, which could effectively solve both the environmental pollution and the energy crisis, so as to achieve “end-of-pipe treatment” and “source control” simultaneously.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are comprised of various organic linkers and metal clusters with inherentfeatures such as a well-ordered porous structure and a large specific surface area, which can provide abundant active sites on the surface and promote the interfacial reaction with reactants, thereby making them widely applied in the field of catalysis [12–14]. Recent studies have been reported that iron-containing MOFs (MIL-101, MIL-88 and MIL-53) were used as photocatalysts for the removal of NOx [15] and Ti-based MOFs NH2-MIL-125(Ti) was applied in the photocatalytic water splitting [16]. Among the known MOFs, NH2-MIL-125(Ti), a well-known candidate with high thermal stability and low toxicity, has been extensively explored for photocatalytic application. However, the photocatalytic activity is always still limited by the weak visible light response and difficult separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs [17–19]. Hence, introducing other functional materials intothe pores of MOFs could be one of numerous feasible strategies to overcome these limitations for improving the photocatalytic performance.

Solar spectrum contain about 47% UV–visible and 53% near-infrared (NIR) light. Through up-conversion effect, we could convert longer wavelength irradiation (e.g., NIR-visible) to shorter wavelength emission (e.g., UV–visible), which could be then re-absorbed and re-used by UV–visible light responsive materials (e.g., NH2-MIL-125(Ti)), thereby making full use of light energy. Carbon nanodots (CDs) are a class of zero-dimensional nanomaterials with unique physical and chemical properties, such as low toxicity, up-conversion photoluminescence property and fast electron transfer capability, which have attracted huge research interest in catalysis, energy conversion and drug delivery [20–22]. Wei et al. fabricated ZIF-8 with CDs as a composite for boosting visible-light-driven photocatalytic removal of nitrogen oxides [23] at room temperature, in which the CDs could act as an significant role in improving the visible light utilization and carrier separation efficiency. Zhang et al. co-incorporated CDs and CdS quantum dots into the pores of MIL-101 to construct CDs/CdS@MIL-101 composites with an excellent photocatalytic H2 generation performance [24] , in which the CDs could serve as an electron collector to extend significantly the lifetime of the photogenerated charge carriers. As we know, a photocatalyst consisted of CDs and MOFs that could simultaneously achieve NO removal and H2 production has not been reported.

In our work, a simple low-temperature calcination means were developed to generate uniformly dispersed CDs in situ with economical glucose within the cages of NH2-MIL-125(Ti) as an ideal template [25,26]. Thus, various CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) were obtained with uniformly distributed CDs in different concentrations of glucose (1, 5, 10, 20 and 30 mM). Moreover, the synergistic effects between the CDs and NH2-MIL-125(Ti) for photocatalytic removal of NO and generation of H2 under illumination was also thoroughly studied. The results showed that CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) (10 mM) can greatly improve the NO removal and H2 evolution rate in comparison with pristine NH2-MIL-125(Ti), indicating that NH2-MIL-125(Ti) encapsulated with in situ-formed CDs could be a feasible method to enhance the photocatalytic activity. To the best of our knowledge, this work was the first to construct a bifunctional catalyst CDs@MOFs that could realize photocatalytic NO removal and H2 evolution simultaneously.

N,N-Dimethylformamide (Adamas, 99%), 2-aminoterephthalic acid (Adamas, 98%), titanium isopropoxide (Aladdin, 99%), glucose(Aladdin, 98%). All reagents were of analytical grade and could be used without further purification.

Based on previous research, NH2-MIL-125(Ti) was obtained by the Nasalevich approach [27]. 2-aminoterephthalic acid and titanium isopropoxide were added in a mixture solution (N,N-dimethylformamide and dry methanol). The mixture solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave for hydrothermal reaction.
Details were shown in the Supplementary material (Text S1).

Based on previous research, CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) was obtained by the Zhang approach [25]. The prepared NH2-MIL-125(Ti) was immersed in the glucose solution and stirred at room temperature. Then the sample was washed several times with EtOH/H2O (9:1) to remove the glucose adsorbed on the surface. Subsequently, the glucose-loaded NH2-MIL-125(Ti) was calcined under a nitrogen stream to obtain CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) (Scheme 1 ). Details were shown in the Supplementary material (Text S2).

Microstructure and morphology of CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) were characterized by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns, Energy dispersive X-Ray spectroscopy (EDX), Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra, Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) experiment, Thermogravimetric (TG) analysis, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) measurements. Photocatalytic properties and mechanisms were investigated by Photoluminescence spectrum (PL spectrum), UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS), Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), Transient photocurrent response curve, Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), Electron spin-resonance spectroscopy (ESR), Upconversion photoluminescence spectra (UCPL) and Diffused reflectance infrared fourier transform spectroscopy (DRIFTS). Details were shown in the Supplementary material (Text S3).

Gas phase catalytic performance was assessed through the removal rate of a low concentration of NO with ppb levels (520 ppb) in a continuous flow reactor (rectangular reactor, volume 4.5L). The 0.1 g catalysts were put into a beaker with 10 mL ethanol, and transferred to a glass dish (12.0 cm in diameter) after ultrasonic dispersion, and dried at 60 °C to test the catalytic activity. An ordinary LED lamp (12 W) was vertically placed outside and above the reactor, and the lamp was turned on when the adsorption desorption equilibrium was achieved. The reactor outlet was connected to a NOx analyzer (Thermo Scientific, 42i-TL). The light source was turned on after the NO concentration became stable, the samples were inspected every 1 min and the results were recorded via the NOx analyzer. The removal rate (η) of NO was calculated by the following formula:
where C and C0 correspond to the concentrations of NO in the outlet and feeding stream, respectively.

Typically, 30 mg catalysts were uniformly dispersed in 17 mL acetonitrile and 0.2 mL deionized water, and 2 mL triethanolamine was used as an electron sacrificial agent in the hydrogen production system. Subsequently, 1 mL acetonitrile solution contained a certain amount of H2PtCl6 was added as a precursor of the cocatalyst Pt (1.25 wt%) in situ photoreduction during the reaction. Then, the reaction solution was bubbled with nitrogen to completely remove the air for at least 0.5 h. Finally, the sealed reactor was irradiated under visible light by using a 300 W Xe lamp equipped with a 380 nm cut-off filter under vigorous stirring and the reaction temperature was always maintained at 10 °C. During the visible light irradiation, the evolved amount of hydrogen generation was collected at given time intervals and analyzed using a GC gas chromatograph (GC 7920, Beijing) equipped with the thermally conductive detector and a high purity N2 carrier gas.

In situ DRIFTS measurements were conducted using a TENSOR II FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker) equipped with an in situ diffuse-reflectance cell (Harrick) and a high-temperature reaction chamber (HVC), as shown in Scheme S1.

Before measurements, the as-prepared samples were placed in a vacuum tube and pretreated 1 h at 110 °C. The background spectra are recorded before injecting NO into the reaction chamber. And the NO absorption bands appear once NO comes in contact with the photocatalyst at 25 °C under dark conditions. Once the adsorption equilibrium is achieved, a visible-light source is applied to initiate the photocatalytic reaction. And the baseline spectra are the same as that of the curve of adsorption equilibrium in NO adsorption process. As for the temporal evolution of normalized absorbance about adsorbed species on photocatalysts surface, the integration of characteristic absorption bands of adsorbed species leads to follow their individual evolutions as a function of time. For all species, the normalized absorbance is calculated by considering their individual maximum absorbance as 1.

From the Fig. 1 a, the peaks of CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) with a less intensity highly matched with pristine NH2-MIL-125(Ti), which proved the porous structure of NH2-MIL-125(Ti) was not affected by the CDs generated in situ with various amounts of glucose after encapsulating in the pores of NH2-MIL-125(Ti). The decreasing peak intensity could as a result of the CDs generated in situ reduce the periodicity of crystal lattices in the MOFs partly. At the same time, the possibility of formingcarbon nanoparticles on the outer surface of the MOFs could also be excluded [26]. In addition, it was worth noting that no significant diffraction peaks were observed from CDs probably due to the amorphous phase or low content. It was found in the Fig. 1b, the IR absorbance peak of CDs at 3461 and 1717 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and CO, thus implying the existence of hydroxy groups. Absorbance peaks at 1402 and 1037 cm−1 corresponded to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of COC, respectively [26]. The absorption peak of CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) at 3461 cm−1 was attributed to the symmetrical vibration of free amino group (NH2) [28], which overlapped with the OH peak of CDs, and the absorption peak observed ambiguously around 1037 cm−1 corresponded to the symmetrical stretching vibration of COC, which could come from the functional groups especially carboxyl group on the surface of CDs generated in situ in the cages of NH2-MIL-125(Ti). However, there was no significant difference between original NH2-MIL-125(Ti) and CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) with different contents of CDs, which may be due to the high dispersion and low content of the CDs in the composites [29].

As shown in Fig. 2 a, Figs. S1 and S2, it could be seen that the morphology of CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) (10 mM) was similar to the morphology of its parent NH2-MIL-125(Ti) through SEM and TEM, which further showed that the content of CDs may not be much combining with XRD and FTIR. In order to confirm the CDs formed in situ via the convenient low-temperature calcination approach, CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) was dispersed in KOH aqueous solution (1 mM) to obtain a suspension of CDs through dissolving the framework of NH2-MIL-125(Ti). Subsequently, the CDs sample was obtained by filtration and extraction with diethyl ether [26]. Some aggregated CDs were observed via the HRTEM (Fig. 2b) of the extracted CDs sample, which indicated that CDs had been generated in situ in the pores of the NH2-MIL-125(Ti) before dissolving the MOFs template. The average particle size of CDs was about 0.65 nm in the pores (Fig. S3), and the CDs were prone to aggregation after dissolving the framework of NH2-MIL-125(Ti), so some aggregated CDs could be observed in Fig. 2b. The EDS element mapping images showed a uniform distribution of C, N, O, Ti elements throughout CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) (Fig. 2c). Moreover, the content of C element in CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) was higher, further confirming the successful loading of CDs (Fig. 2(d-e)).

The chemical composition and electronic structure of the CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) was investigated by XPS. The XPS data showed two major peaks at 285.5 and 532 eV, which were attributed to C1s and O1s (Fig. S6). The C1s spectrum of the CDs indicated three types of carbon bonds: C = C (284.8 eV), C-O (286.3 eV) and C–C (283.3 eV). As shown in Fig. 3 a, the peak of C1s spectrum could be divided into the peaks at 284.75, 285.85, 286.43, and 288.79 eV in pristine NH2-MIL-125(Ti), which were assigned to CC, CN, CC, and CO groups, respectively [30]. However, in the C1s spectrum of CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti), it was seen that the peaks of CC and CN shifted approximately 0.35 eV and 0.23 eV to high binding energy respectively. At the same time, the –NH+- group (402.1 eV) [31,32] and the peak of C-N shifted about 0.44 eV to low binding energy in the peak of N1s spectrum (Fig. 3b), further indicating the interactions could exist between NH2 and CDs.

Thermogravimetric analysis under N2 atmosphere showed that NH2-MIL-125(Ti) could keep the frame structure below 310 °C (Fig. S8 (a)), which suggested the glucose molecules were converted into an array of carbon dots along with the stable NH2-MIL-125(Ti) during the low-temperature calcination process (240 °C for 2 h in an N2 atmosphere), and the color change from yellow to yellowish brown was a significant manifestation after calcination (Fig. S8 (b-c)). According to the TGA results, the final content of CDs in CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) (10 mM) was about 2.7 wt% [26].

The porosity of the materials was discussed by N2 adsorption–desorption experiments (Fig. S9). Based on the N2 adsorption results, the BET surface areas of NH2-MIL-125(Ti) and CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) (0 mM, 1 mM, 5 mM, 10 mM, 20 mM, 30 mM) were about 1449.57, 1206.26, 1275.12, 1312.13, 1401.79, and 1347.22 m2 g−1 respectively, and the corresponding pore volume distributions were 0.549, 0.463, 0.483, 0.499, 0.531 and 0.520 cm3 g−1, respectively. The pore volume of CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) were smaller, suggesting there was a small amount of CDs was formed in the pore of NH2-MIL-125(Ti). The pore size of NH2-MIL-125(Ti) was about 0.678 nm. As mentioned earlier, the average particle size of CDs was about 0.65 nm, which was very consistent with the pore size of NH2-MIL-125(Ti), further proving that CDs were in situ generated in the pores of NH2-MIL-125(Ti).

The UV–visible absorption spectrum was determined to obtain definite regarding the feasible principle of high photocatalytic activity of CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti). As shown in Fig. 4 a, it was apparent that the visible absorption region and intensity of CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) were increasing than that of the original NH2-MIL-125(Ti), which could be due to the n-π* and π-π* transitions of the carbonized species from glucose. With an increasing amount of CDs added to the NH2-MIL-125(Ti), the optical response of the composite was generally improved. However, the sample with the higher encapsulation of CDs (20 mM, 30 mM) had a lower light absorption than 10 mM. It could be due to that excessive encapsulation of CDs inside the NH2-MIL-125(Ti) affected the absorption and utilization rate of light [33]. In addition, the band gaps of CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) with various amounts of glucose (0 mM, 1 mM, 5 mM, 10 mM, 20 mM, and 30 mM) were 2.64, 2.49, 2.47, 2.42, 2.58 and 2.52 eV, respectively, in Fig. 4b. The above results indicated that the CDs were an outstanding optical response material encapsulated in NH2-MIL-125(Ti) were able to increase the utilization of light energy and reduce band gap [34].

In comparison with pristine MOFs, the luminous intensity of CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) was reduced in Fig. 5 a, indicating the CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) had afastchargetransfer ability. The result demonstrated that CDs could act as an efficient electronic reservoir and block the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers under illumination. In addition, when the glucose concentration was higher than 10 mM, the recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs increased, indicating excessive encapsulation of CDs was disadvantageous for charge separation, which was in line with the results of the photocatalytic activity. It could be seen from the EIS (Fig. 5b) that the arc radius of CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) (10 mM) was the smallest, implying a certain amount of CDs could significantly reduce the charge transfer resistance (Rct). The separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers could be estimated by the transient photocurrent response under illumination. As shown in Fig. 5c, transient photocurrent response curve of CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) (10 mM) was obviously the strongest, which showed the separation efficiency of charge carrier was improved along with CDs loading. Linear scanning voltammetry (LSV) analysis was also illustrated in Fig. 5d. The strongest current density of CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) (10 mM) was observed, which obviously displayed the CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) (10 mM) was more responsive to the photovoltage.

As shown in Fig. 6 , the CDs could emit a shorter wave-length light of 350–550 nm when it was excited by light with a wavelength of 600–800 nm, and the emitted light could cover part of the ultraviolet region, which demonstrated that the prepared CDs formed in situ have up-conversion effects. The up-conversion photoluminescence of CDs could be attributed to the multiphoton active process, in which the simultaneous absorption of two or more photons would result in an emission wavelength shorter than the excitation wavelength (anti-Stokes type emission) [23,35]. Therefore, the up-conversion effects of CDs displayed the CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) could absorb more visible light even near IR spectrum and further enhance the photocatalytic performance, which suggested that the CDs may play an important role in driving NH2-MIL-125(Ti) into more visible light response.

As observed from the activity tests (Fig. 7 a), the NO elimination efficiency of original NH2-MIL-125(Ti) only reached 24.6% under visible light irradiation, which was much lower than that of CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) with different CDs encapsulation. The optimal removal efficiency reached about 53% of the initial nitrogen oxides for CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) (10 mM), suggesting excessive CDs was not conducive to enhancing photocatalytic performance. The reason for that phenomenon could be over-loaded CDs may shield the illumination and serve as possible recombination centers, further decreasing photocatalytic activity. The photocatalytic NO removal ratio of CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) (10 mM) was far exceeding that of commercial P25 (about 20.2%) under visible light irradiation, indicating the optimized CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) (10 mM) had a great potential in photocatalytic degradation pollution. Morever, the NO2 yield was further probed (Fig. S11) as the NO2 was highly toxic, which could be produced by the partial oxidation of NO. It could be seen that CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) only produce a small amount of NO2, which indicated the secondary pollution was avoided. In addition, the stability of the photocatalyst was also tested. After 5 cycles, the activity was almost not reduced, implying the CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) composite catalyst could serve as a stable and efficient photocatalyst to implement the NO elimination (Fig. 7b).

It was found in Fig. 7c, the photocatalytic H2 evolution rates for CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) with various initial amounts of glucose (1 mM, 5 mM, 10 mM, 20 mM, and 30 mM) were 3115.76, 4544.61, 5820.95, 3340.91, 2830.88 μmol g−1 respectively, which was higher than that of original NH2-MIL-125(Ti) (1090.09 μmol g−1), suggesting the presence of CDs was critical to improving photocatalytic H2 generation activity and a synergistic effect should exist between the CDs and NH2-MIL-125(Ti). At the same time, a histogram of H2 produced by NH2-MIL-125(Ti) and CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) on average per hour was also given in Fig. S12 (a). In addition, the photocatalytic H2 evolution rates increased along with the increasing loading of CDs encapsulation atthebeginning, and then decreased after the CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) with initial amount of glucose (10 mM), suggesting the excessive CDs was also not conducive to enhancing photocatalytic H2 generation performance as the NO removal. As shown in Fig. 7d, the CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) (10 mM) was calcined at a series of different temperatures (200, 220, 240, 260, 280 °C) respectively, in which the CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) (10 mM) calcinated at 240 °C displayed the best photocatalytic hydrogen production activity. However, after three cycles, the photocatalytic activity of CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) (10 mM) showed a gradually decreasing cycle performance in Fig. S12 (b), which needed to further make a profound study in our future work.

We further evaluated the stability and recoverability of CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) (10 mM) after 5 cycles (photocatalytic removal of NO) and 3 cycles (photocatalytic hydrogen production), respectively. As shown in the Figs. S13–S15, after multiple catalytic evaluation processes, we could find that there was no obvious difference of the XRD between CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) (10 mM) and NH2-MIL-125(Ti), indicating that the crystal form was stable. In addition, the SEM images showed that the surface of CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) (10 mM) became rougher after multiple catalytic evaluation processes. The BET surface area and pore volume of the catalyst decreased after multiple catalytic evaluation processes, especially the BET surface area of the catalyst was only 373.34 (m2/g) after photocatalytic hydrogen production (it could be that some small organic molecules entered the pores of MOFs), which could result in the gradually decreasing cycle performance of photocatalytic hydrogen production. However, CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) (10 mM) still maintained a large BET surface area after photocatalytic NO removal, which could result in the relatively stable catalytic cycle performance during the photocatalytic NO removal process.

In order to visually and dynamically display the mechanism of photocatalytic NO removal, the in situ DRIFTS measurement was used to explore intermediate species during the reaction process over the illumination time. In the dark environment, along with the mixed gas (NO + O2) flowed without an interval in the reaction vessel, the adsorption–desorption of NO achieved balance by inches, the typical bands of 892 cm−1 (N2O3) [36], 937 cm−1 (NO2) [37], 953 cm−1 (NO2) [37], 968 cm−1 (N2O3) [36], 986 cm−1 (chelating bidentate nitrates) [38], 1016 cm−1 (trans-N2O2) [39], 1064 cm−1 (NO3 −) [40], 1090 cm−1 (NO) [39], 1125 cm−1 (bidentate nitrites) [38] and 1160 cm−1 (NO) [41] were noticed in the Fig. 8 a (as shown by the black dotted line). In Fig. 8b, the bands of 968 cm−1 and 1125 cm−1 were strengthened a little, the band of 986 cm−1 was red-shifted to 992 cm−1. At the same time, we also could find there were two additional bands, which were corresponded to 906 cm−1 (NO2 −) [42] and 1051 cm−1 (cis-N2O2) [39], respectively. The results revealed that the oxidation ability of CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) could be better than that of NH2-MIL-125(Ti). Some possible conversion pathways of NO on NH2-MIL-125(Ti) and CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) could be deduced by the equations: 2NO + 2e− → 2NO− → cis-N2O2 2− and NO + O2 + e− → NO3 − [40]. After illumination in Fig. 8c, the bands of 968 cm−1, 1016 cm−1, and 1064 cm−1 were evidently enhanced, and the band of 892 cm−1 was red-shifted to 896 cm−1, the bands of 937 cm−1 and 953 cm−1 were blue-shift to 930 cm−1 and 947 cm−1, respectively. In addition, there were three additional bands appeared at 980 cm−1 (NO) [41], 1047 cm−1 (cis-N2O2) [39] and 1102 cm−1 (nitrite) [43]. In Fig. 8d, the intensity of infrared bands at 892 cm−1, 968 cm−1, 1051 cm−1, 1125 cm−1 and 1160 cm−1 were significantly enhanced with the increase of time, indicating that NO was being oxidized under illumination. Compared with Fig. 8c, with illumination, the band at 1125 cm−1 in Fig. 8d became stronger, the band at 1160 cm−1 became weaker, and there was an extra band at 906 (NO2 −) cm−1 [41], the band at 980 cm−1 disappeared, suggesting that the oxidation ability of CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) to NO was better. The NO3 − intensity increased on NH2-MIL-125(Ti) and CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) could be attributed to the reactive oxygen species (ROS) (
O2 −,
OH) participating in photocatalytic oxidation: NO/N2O3 + ROS → NO2 −/NO3 − [40].

The 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) acted as the spin trapping agent to explore the possible active species generated during the photocatalytic reaction through ESR measurement. It was found in Fig. 9 a, the ESR spectrum showed four typical peaks increased sharply after illumination with an intensity ratio of 1: 1: 1: 1, which were representative peaks of
O2
− /DMPO composite, and the intensity of these peaks became stronger along with the increasing illumination time, revealing the formation of superoxide radicals (
O2
− ). Similarly, there were also four typical peaks with approximate signal intensity of 1: 2: 2:1 in Fig. 9b, which proved the production of hydroxyl radicals (
OH). However, NH2-MIL-125(Ti) and CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) had almost no
OH and
O2 − signals when the lamp was not turned on, indicating the light was very critical to obtain the active species. The signals of active radicals came from CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) were more obvious, which displayed the CDs could boost NH2-MIL-125(Ti) to generate more active radicals under illumination. Based on the ESR tests, the prime active species (
OH and
O2
− radicals) could be clearly formed during the photocatalysis process through fabricating CDs with NH2-MIL-125(Ti) under illumination.

To further make clear that the CDs play a crucial role for the enhanced photocatalytic performance, the photocatalytic activity of NH2-MIL-125(Ti) and CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) under different monochromatic light irradiation was measured (Fig. S11). Under 365 nm UV light irradiation, NH2-MIL-125(Ti) showed a NO removal efficiency of 24.2%, while CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) had an activity of 32%. As we know, the up-conversion or photosensitization effect of CDs would not work under 365 nm UV light irradiation. Therefore, the result also proved CDs could act as electron mediator to promote the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs, which was consistent with transient photocurrent response and EIS. In addition, the NH2-MIL-125(Ti) showed almost no activity under 500 nm of monochromatic light irradiation, however, CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) had an activity of 8.4%, which indicated the CDs could increase the excited electron-hole pairs through up-conversion and photosensitization effect. Therefore, the introduction of CDs not only could promote the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs but also could increase the excited electron-hole pairs under visible light irradiation, thereby resulting in enhanced photocatalytic performance [23].

It was well known that the strong visible light response and high separation efficiency of photo-generated electron-hole pairs were conducive to photocatalytic performance. For MOFs, the organic linkers (HOMO) could act as VB and the metal clusters (LUMO) corresponding to CB, respectively [44]. Combined above the NO removal and H2 evolution results with corresponding characterization analysis of the innovative bifunctional photocatalyst, especially UV–Vis, UCPL and in situ DRIFTS, the possible photocatalytic pathways (formulas (1)-(10)) and schematic model (scheme 2) of the CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) were speculated: CDs absorbed long-wavelength light (e.g., NIR-visible) and emitted short-wavelength light (e.g., UV–visible) through the up-conversion effect, which expanded the light absorption range of CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) and effectively separated photogenerated electron-hole pairs (formula (1)). In photocatalytic NO removal system, hydroxide (OH−) adsorbed on the CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) surface could be oxidized by the photo-generated holes (h+) to form hydroxyl radical (
OH) (formula (2)) [33], the photo-generated electrons (e−) with reducibility could react with O2 adsorbed on the surface of the photocatalyst to form
O2 − (formula (3)) or further transform into
OH (formulas (4)-(5)), which would synergistically convert NO to NO3 − finally (formulas (6)-(8)) [45]. With the aid of co-catalyst in photocatalytic H2 production system, the photo-generated electron (e−) was captured by H+ to produce H2 (formulas (9)–(10)). Among both of removal of NO and H2 production, the CDs generated in situ could act as a very valuable role in light harvesting and charge separation.
Possible photocatalytic pathways:

CDs (up-conversion): λNIR-visible → λUV–visible
(1) NH2-MIL-125(Ti) → e− + h+
(2) h+ + OH− →
OH
(3) e− + O2 →
O2 −
(4)
O2 − + 2H+ + e− → H2O2
(5) H2O2 + e− →
OH + OH−
(6)
O2 − + NO → NO3 −
(7) 2
OH + NO → NO2 + H2O
(8) NO2 +
OH → NO3 − + H+
(9) H2O → OH− + H+
(10) 2e− + 2H+ → H2
Schematic model:

In summary, a simple and convenient low-temperature calcination approach was successfully adopted to fabricate a novel dual-functional photocatalyst CDs@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) with the CDs encapsulated in situ within the pores of NH2-MIL-125(Ti) as an ideal template, which could effectively achieve photocatalytic removal of NO and H2 production simultaneously. The CDs in situ-formed in the pores of NH2-MIL-125(Ti) could uniformly dispersed in the pores of NH2-MIL-125(Ti) and more easily in contact with the central metal cluster, which could facilitate the photogenerated charge carriers transfer to the central metal cluster. In addition, the NH2 of NH2-MIL-125(Ti) could interact with the OH/COOH on the surface of the CDs through chemical bond forces such as hydrogen bond, which could facilitate the CDs closer to the central metal cluster to improve the efficiency of the up-conversion or photosensitization effect of CDs. The results of this study provided a possible choice to integrate CDs with MOFs to fabricate dual-functional photocatalysts to simultaneously achieve the photocatalytic NO eliminate and H2 evolution. This strategy is expected as an alternative approach for the design and synthesis of bifunctional composite catalysts to simultaneously enhance the oxidation and reduction capacity.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21502012, 21805027), the Chongqing Science and Technology Commission (cstc2018jcyjAX0531, cstc2017jcyjAX0404), the Scientific and Technological Research Program of Chongqing Municipal Education Commission (KJQN201800836, KJQN201900838, KJ1500615, KJ1706174), the Funding Project for Scientific Research of Chongqing Technology and Business University (1752015, 1952004), the Innovation group of new technologies for industrial pollution control of Chongqing Education Commission (CXQT19023), the program of China Scholarships Council (201906075005), the Chongqing Key Laboratory of Catalysis and New Environmental Materials (KFJJ2019084), the Chongqing College Students’ Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program (S201911799027).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2020.127643.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facet-dependent photocatalytic NO conversion pathways predetermined by adsorption activation patterns

B Environ. 205 (2017) 532–540. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apcatb.2017.01.009.
